speaker: interviewer
text: This is Survivors of the Shoah Visual History Interview with Henry Frankel. Henry Frankel. Interviewer's name is David Brotsky. The interview is taking place in North Bergen, New Jersey in the United States of America on September 30, 1996. The language of the interview is English. This is David Brotsky and today I'm interviewing Henry Frankel. Can you tell us your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Henry Frankel.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Spell-- spell it? F-R-A-N-K-E-L. My previous name-- I was born with F-R-E-N-K-I-E-L. The name was changed during naturalization when I came to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any other names? Any false names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only name that I was working in Warsaw for the underground was Tadek, spelled T-A-D-E-K. I only responded to this name when I was outside of the ghetto. There no other names that I responded to or knew that I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your Hebrew name? What's your Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name was given to me by the organization of the Polish Socialist Party, the PPS, spelled in Polish, [POLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born June 16, 1924 in Lodz, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Hayim Mayer, but they were calling him Max Frenkiel. He was born in December 12, in 1888.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always having time, interested in our school, work and participating. Encouraging us to join Zionist organizations. And particularly, was very fond of us when we got together on holidays and had some other people on Shabbat celebrating Shabbat in holidays in a real Jewish way.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had the textile manufacturing place, quite a large one that he built up himself. And he had a-- his brother was a partner to the finished goods that the plant was in [NON-ENGLISH], near Lodz. 10 kilometers north east-- north east-- northwest of Lodz. And they distribute-- they-- a place of the business of his father, of my father, brother, and him was in Lodz. On Narutowicza and Piortkowska Street in Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that street? How do you spell the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: N-A-R-U-T-O-W-I-C-Z-A. Narutowicza Street in Lodz, and Piortkowska Street, from the name of city of Piotrkow. P-I-O-R-T-K-O-W-S-K-A Street.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of factory was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was textiles, weaving and finishing. And a quite successful business. I didn't really experience any hardship at home. And my brother and my-- and me were attending private, Jewish gymnasium, predominantly of interest to father for Hebrew studies. I attended the gymnasium from 1934 till 19-- till the outbreak of war in Poland, September 1, 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Salomea Frenkiel, spelled F-R-E-N-K-I-E-L, but maiden name was Slomnicki, from the family of Slomnicki in Warsaw. She originally was from Warsaw, a participant in quite a few drives for the needy children and, and a real, true mother. Interested in developing children, and the benefit of both my, myself and my my brother and myself. Always interested in our studies and our progress in learning.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of-- what was she like?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was very warm, an extremely loving mother with the-- knowing, with the knowledge of developing a good relation between the children-- son, the son and mother. And always looking after people coming and having good company for us for the benefit of the developing gifts. I also remember that from time to time, there were people from orphanages invited for Shabbats, to our house to enjoy the Shabbat meal, and staying over for going together to shul, and so on. Mother was even participating in Warsaw when there was shortages of food and things like this. If there was any possibility of giving and sharing with others, she always gave with the utmost.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your brother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother's name was Mieczyslaw, spelled M-I-E-C-Z-Y-S-L-A-W. Mieczyslaw Frenkiel. His Jewish name I don't remember very well, but I think it was [NON-ENGLISH]. He departed-- when he graduated from the Jewish gymnasium in Lodz, he departed for studies of textile manufacturing to, to Belgium, where he finished after the war. The city that he departed for [NON-ENGLISH] in Belgium. And he's living, still today, over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did he leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: He left Poland in 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born on January 26, 1918. In Warsaw. He was a very good student, extremely talented when it comes to coloring and recognizing shades in coloring. And that's how my father decided that there should be an engineer in a family for textile industry. During the years of war, he spent in prison or war camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we'll talk about that later on. Was your dad home a lot or was he working--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Whenever he didn't have to [? become, ?] or visit, or attend any business meetings, he was mostly home. And they were like Sundays. They were absolutely free. We were going to soccer matches and some morning events. There were, during the week, some concert or some Jewish entertainment, cultural events taking place in Lodz. And we were always participating in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of those specifically?

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the-- there was a famous [NON-ENGLISH] of [NON-ENGLISH] of-- in Lodz. And I don't remember exactly the names of the participating [INAUDIBLE], but this was one of the foremost events, cultural event of the Jewish life in Europe. This group later became the group of Tel Aviv, theatrical group, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of religious upbringing did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm. There was-- we observed quite well all the holidays, and there was [NON-ENGLISH] in the house and we were brought up in it. A [NON-ENGLISH] is something that you can live with. There's nothing wrong with it, and you can participate and enjoy it. This, you brought it up from the home. There was no struggle about it. However, there too are people who will-- don't know how to deal with it and they give up before they even start.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Shabbats like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very traditional Shabbats with lighting of candles and our standing with a father who came from shul. And standing up, and my mother lit up the candles. We were standing in-- paying attention to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a special meal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Of course, there was fish and the special kugel and delicacies that we always enjoy. Mother knew precisely which one of us liked what and this was on the table. The funny part of it was that the children that we invited from the orphanages were always surprised how well we did remember what did they like, and was excited about.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have anyone living in the house? And the maids or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There were just me and my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember some of your other relatives living in town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have cousins of my father's brother that I was visiting. And one of them was attending-- two of them actually, were attending the same gymnasium of-- where my brother was. The first Jewish gymnasium in Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that called?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was called the First Jewish Gymnasium of Lodz. And there were two of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two gymnasium of the same-- First and Second Jewish Gymnasium of Lodz. Gymnasium means high school that you receive your matriculation. You could continue to further studies in colleges. I was attending the gymnasium of Itzhak Katzenelson, poet, and teacher, and educator.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first start going to school? What school did you first go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was attending first a public school, polish public school, until the age of 11, I think. And then I was sent to the gymnasium of Itzhak Katzenelson in Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the public school like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was mixed crowd, predominantly Jewish, and also Christian. But there was a very outstanding relation. There was no, no bickering or, fighting, or-- [COUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --of any sort. Did you have non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [INAUDIBLE] friends, and they were very nice, very kind. They were many times coming to our house and we're going to their house. And there was in case like this, there was the kibitzing of us that you can't eat anything go here because it is not kosher. And they knew about kosher and things like this. They knew who observed, who did not. But the relation remained, throughout the public schools, quite good, even in the later stages of the six and seven class of the final years of public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about the teachers?

speaker: interviewee
text: The teachers were mixed. They were Polish and Jewish. Yeah. Very devoted to, to the classes, and they did enjoy when they getting a response from the class of absorbing the material that they were delivering.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you switched to another school?

speaker: interviewee
text: To gymnasium. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that you switch, but if you wanted to continue, either you could go after public school to a vocational school and learn a trade, or you wanted to continue in studies, they you continued through gymnasium, and lyceum and then to university. The gymnasium had four years of classes, including Latin, and Hebrew, and [NON-ENGLISH] and Jewish history. And Latin was needed because it was compulsory curriculum of the Polish government. And after the four years, you received the small matriculation papers, and you could continue to lyceum, the final two years for preparation for the colleges or universities. And with good grades, you did not have to go through examination to enter any of the universities. You were automatically accepted. Even in some of the universities outside of the Poland, there were accepting people from our schools in good-- with good grades.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the school different? How did you find-- how did you find the school to be different from the public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the public? Or, it's a quite great amount of learning, and we did not have the possibility of learning of algebra, geometry, trigonometry, history, geography. These things were lumped together with-- history and geography were combined into one unit that you learned about the part of the world and there was history involved. So it was arranged in such a way that you could absorb this material, providing that you wanted to learn.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was where? In the public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That was in gymnasium. In the public school, there was a certain amount of pressure, but not with the same amount as you experience in probably in the gymnasium, in the high school. Our high school were rigorous. There were not allowances of any excuses, of headaches, and finger aches or something else. If there was anything, you had to bring the doctor's certificate for it. Parents did not collaborate very often. They wanted the children to learn that there is discipline and they had to go to school to learn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you meet Katzenelson? Did you meet--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Itzhak.

speaker: interviewee
text: Katzenelson was our teacher of Hebrew and of course, whenever he wrote a poem in Hebrew, he was trying to teach us to remember it. And primarily, he was writing songs for children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he well known then, at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he well known before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. He was, quite well. He was number of times to, to Palestine prior to establishing the state of Israel. And he was fluent in Hebrew, and he expected every one of his students to become fluent in Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I developed Hebrew much later. I-- I just was coping with my necessary demand in school. And even though father helped us out-- father knew Hebrew and was trying to help us out. My brother knew Hebrew much better than I, and yet, I somehow sort of went through the high school with the knowledge of Hebrew that I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the-- what type of person was Katzenelson like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Katzenelson went to Warsaw, and the Germans knew about him. They were trying to corral him on several occasions without any corporation. He was not deported during the 1942-- deportation of Jews from Warsaw. He remained in Lodz-- he remained in Warsaw until the uprising. I did remember seeing him sometime in 1943 in February, and then I didn't see him again.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about before the war though?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war? Before the war, he was just a normal-- think-- any celebration in school, in the, in the gymnasium court, where they were in celebration or such thing as some holidays or thing, or event, sport event, he was always there. And he always knew his people by his first name. And they called his pupils by the-- students by the first name. It brings only a good memory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people respect him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. They loved him. Whenever they have seen that Itzhak is coming, there was immediately attention paid to him. There was a response that you don't see it quite often nowadays in schools. Where these were the-- teachers see-- the teacher-- when the children see the teacher, they walk away. There was no such thing with us. We loved our teacher. We knew who he was and what did he expect us to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often would he teach you Hebrew?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had four times a week, Hebrew, intensive care, intensive studies. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And he taught it all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hebrew. he was sometimes replaced by-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] A teacher by name, Bunin, spelled B-U-N-I-N. Whatever happened to this man, I don't know. But I didn't see him in Warsaw when I was-- when we came to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened? You were in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was just as good, as friendly, and outgoing individual. Very pleasant approach with student that-- especially in the final year in gymnasium, we felt there was a certain friendship relation. [INAUDIBLE]. There was not-- that's just like teaching someone something, but he would sit down with us and try to incite us to speak in Hebrew to him, and he would respond slowly so we can absorb it with great--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bunin and, and Katzenelson. Extremely friendly people. And I was-- it was very fun talking about them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a big school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they had-- quite a large school for the size of the building that occupy. The building is still standing today in Lodz. It was, of course, confiscated by the Polish government and turned into a medical school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is it located? On Zawadzka Street in Lodz, spelled Z-A-W-A-D-Z-K-A. 43. And did you live near there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were living-- I had to take a tram and then, and another tram. So there was something like, 20 blocks away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, where did-- where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were living on Lealee Koszcuiczo, eight.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the other place was in [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that. Lealee is L-E-A-L-E-E. Kosciusko K-O-S-Z-C-U-I-C-Z-K-O. Eight. I know it's not an easy language to, to explain to people how to spell it, but to people who understand Polish, it will be very easy absorb it. And when did you go to school there till?

speaker: interviewee
text: From 8:00 to 4:00 in the afternoon and sometimes till 5:00 afternoon. Saturdays were off, but we had school on Sunday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hebrew studies or both?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Both Hebrew and English on Sunday?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There were Hebrew, and Polish, and Latin was compulsory.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- what age did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- not until I graduated from this, what they called, small high school, the small gymnasium. And all I had to do is get two more years of lyceum for preparation of-- for the high studies in colleges. The war broke out in 1939, and I couldn't continue. There were no possibilities of anybody to study in any high school or anything like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do remember before the war broke out, did you have any anti-Semitism that you experienced?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yeah. There was quite a number of incidents in Poland and Lodz, despite the fact that the quite large percentage of Jews were living in Lodz. I'd like to stress the fact that Lodz had something slightly over 600,000 inhabitants, and about 49% were Jewish. So this was quite a large community of Jewish population. And yet, there were anti-Semitic outbreaks in some parts of Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- not personally. I was always surrounded with friends of Jewish faith, and I could not say that I experienced it. But I know quite a bit, quite a few things. And you read in the papers, in some small towns, how badly things were mistreated. There was quite a famous priest, by name Trzecziak, who was just getting simply, absolutely, mad when he could spot a Jew in his vicinity or in place near him. And he incited people to conduct himself in very unhuman manner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see him in person?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met Trzecziak in Warsaw by accident when I was-- when I entered the Cathedral of the Three Crosses. There was a different man then. He was very polite. He-- when I walked in and he knew that I was Jewish, and he received me very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1941, in winter of 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Very, very hard name to spell. T-R-Z-E-C-Z-I-A-K. Trzecziak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any dealings with him that time you met him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. This was accidental need for stepping inside and receiving some warm water because of the severe cold winter in 1941. And I received the cold water and some piece of bread with some tea and, then I left without being-- becoming a nuisance or asking for more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, do you remember as the war was coming, did you hear things before the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm. Actually, prior to the outbreak of World War II, there were many Jewish people from Germany shipped to Poland, especially the one that had built a connection with Poland. And they were expelled from Germany for being Polish subjects. And whatever they were experiencing, that somehow people could not believe that this thing can happen. And there was done in such a brutal manner. They just asked them to pack up whatever they could, and take it with them, and leave. Even the people that really know that this is true, whatever they went through, they did not respond to it with full faith until they was too late.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your opinion of Germany before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Disappointing that a country like-- described by so many people of culture, and especially music, and poetry, and so on, and so one, could come up with-- could deliver such unusual blows to humanity.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear a lot of what had happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were reading and we have seen in the movies, on the short news, what was going on in Germany, this Kristallnacht and the other things. And we were absolutely upset and-- but still, there was certain security that we did not want to accept that this is the truth. And of course, later on, things happened.
